answer:
Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Rate Limiters at the backend level covers implementation patterns (token bucket, sliding window, leaky bucket), distributed rate limiting across multiple server instances, per-route and per-user granularity, graceful degradation when the rate limiter backend (Redis) is unavailable, and trade-offs between precision and performance.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj implemented tiered rate limiting: (1) Global: 10K req/min across all instances (DDoS protection), (2) Per-user: 100 req/min for employees, 500 for managers, 2000 for admins, (3) Per-endpoint: leave submission limited to 10/min (prevent accidental duplicates), file upload limited to 5/min (expensive operation). All limits enforced via Redis sliding window across 4 Fastify instances.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Lua script for atomic sliding window in Redis
const SLIDING_WINDOW_SCRIPT = `
local key = KEYS[1]
local window_ms = tonumber(ARGV[1])
local max_requests = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local now = tonumber(ARGV[3])
local window_start = now - window_ms

-- Remove expired entries
redis.call('ZREMRANGEBYSCORE', key, 0, window_start)

-- Count current window
local count = redis.call('ZCARD', key)

if count >= max_requests then
  -- Get oldest entry to calculate retry-after
  local oldest = redis.call('ZRANGE', key, 0, 0, 'WITHSCORES')
  local retry_after = 0
  if #oldest > 0 then
    retry_after = math.ceil((tonumber(oldest[2]) + window_ms - now) / 1000)
  end
  return {0, count, retry_after}
end

-- Add current request
redis.call('ZADD', key, now, now .. ':' .. math.random())
redis.call('PEXPIRE', key, window_ms + 1000)

return {1, count + 1, 0}
`;

async function slidingWindowRateLimit(
  redis: RedisClient,
  key: string,
  windowMs: number,
  maxRequests: number,
): Promise<{ allowed: boolean; current: number; retryAfter: number }> {
  const result = await redis.eval(SLIDING_WINDOW_SCRIPT, {
    keys: [key],
    arguments: [windowMs.toString(), maxRequests.toString(), Date.now().toString()],
  }) as [number, number, number];

  return {
    allowed: result[0] === 1,
    current: result[1],
    retryAfter: result[2],
  };
}

// Multi-layer rate limiting middleware
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
  const userId = req.user?.id || req.ip;
  const role = req.user?.role || 'anonymous';
  const endpoint = `${req.method}:${req.routeOptions?.url || req.url}`;

  // Layer 1: Per-user limit
  const userLimit = ROLE_LIMITS[role] || { windowMs: 60000, max: 50 };
  const userResult = await slidingWindowRateLimit(
    redis, `rl:user:${userId}`, userLimit.windowMs, userLimit.max,
  );

  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Limit', userLimit.max);
  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Remaining', Math.max(0, userLimit.max - userResult.current));

  if (!userResult.allowed) {
    reply.header('Retry-After', userResult.retryAfter);
    throw fastify.httpErrors.tooManyRequests('User rate limit exceeded');
  }

  // Layer 2: Per-endpoint limit (only for specific endpoints)
  const endpointLimit = ENDPOINT_LIMITS[endpoint];
  if (endpointLimit) {
    const epResult = await slidingWindowRateLimit(
      redis, `rl:ep:${userId}:${endpoint}`, endpointLimit.windowMs, endpointLimit.max,
    );
    if (!epResult.allowed) {
      throw fastify.httpErrors.tooManyRequests(`Endpoint rate limit: ${endpoint}`);
    }
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: An HR admin reported that they were rate-limited while legitimately processing 200 year-end leave adjustments. Meanwhile, an automated script was hammering the leave search endpoint at 1000 req/min without being throttled.

Investigation: Manoj checked the rate limiter config. The admin role was correctly set to 2000 req/min, but the leave adjustment endpoint had a per-endpoint limit of 10/min (same as leave submission — wrong copy-paste). The search endpoint had no per-endpoint limit, and the global per-user limit of 2000 req/min wasn't triggered by the script because it used 5 different API keys.

Fix: (1) Created separate per-endpoint configs: leave-submit 10/min, leave-adjust 200/min, search 500/min. (2) Added IP-based rate limiting as a secondary layer (5000 req/min per IP regardless of user). (3) Made rate limits configurable via Redis hash (admin can update without deploy). (4) Added a monitoring dashboard showing rate limit hits by user, endpoint, and IP.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
One-size-fits-all limits block legitimate useRole-based + endpoint-specific + configurable limits
Redis down disables rate limiting entirelyLocal token bucket fallback with conservative limits
Distributed bypass via multiple accounts/IPsMulti-layer: user + IP + global; rate limit at each layer
Rate check adds latency to every requestLua script for atomic Redis operation (single round-trip)

Validation Strategy

  • Precision test: send exactly max+1 requests in window, verify last one rejected
  • Distributed test: send requests from 4 different instances, verify global count correct
  • Fallback test: kill Redis, verify local rate limiter kicks in within 1 second
  • Config change test: update limit via Redis, verify takes effect within 1 request

Definition

Architecting Rate Limiters for 1M+ users requires rate limiting infrastructure that handles millions of rate checks per second, uses memory efficiently for per-user state, supports dynamic configuration changes, and provides accurate limiting across distributed server instances without becoming a bottleneck itself.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled ContactsHub with 1M users, Manoj architects a 3-tier rate limiting system: (1) Edge: Cloudflare rate limiting at 1000 req/min per IP (no backend load), (2) Application: Redis-based sliding window per user (free: 60 req/min, premium: 600 req/min), (3) Resource-based: Cloudinary operations capped at 100/hour, MongoDB write operations at 5000/hour per user. Peak traffic: 50K concurrent users × 2 req/s = 100K rate checks/second.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Hybrid rate limiter: local fast-path + Redis precise check
import { LRUCache } from 'lru-cache';

// Local token bucket cache (per-instance, not shared)
const localBuckets = new LRUCache({
  max: 100000, // Track 100K users locally
  ttl: 120000, // 2 min TTL
});

function localQuickCheck(userId: string, maxTokens: number): 'allow' | 'check_redis' {
  const now = Date.now();
  let bucket = localBuckets.get(userId);

  if (!bucket) {
    bucket = { tokens: maxTokens, lastRefill: now };
    localBuckets.set(userId, bucket);
  }

  // Refill tokens based on elapsed time
  const elapsed = (now - bucket.lastRefill) / 60000; // minutes
  bucket.tokens = Math.min(maxTokens, bucket.tokens + elapsed * maxTokens);
  bucket.lastRefill = now;

  if (bucket.tokens > maxTokens * 0.5) {
    // User has plenty of budget — allow without Redis check
    bucket.tokens -= 1;
    return 'allow';
  }

  // Close to limit — need Redis precision
  return 'check_redis';
}

// Rate limit middleware with fast path
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
  const userId = req.user?.id || req.ip;
  const tier = req.user?.tier || 'free';
  const limit = TIER_LIMITS[tier];

  // Fast path: local check (sub-microsecond, no I/O)
  const quickResult = localQuickCheck(userId, limit.max);
  if (quickResult === 'allow') {
    reply.header('X-RateLimit-Remaining', 'plenty');
    return;
  }

  // Slow path: Redis precise check (0.5ms)
  const result = await slidingWindowRateLimit(
    redis, `rl:${userId}`, limit.windowMs, limit.max,
  );

  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Limit', limit.max);
  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Remaining', Math.max(0, limit.max - result.current));

  if (!result.allowed) {
    reply.header('Retry-After', result.retryAfter);
    throw fastify.httpErrors.tooManyRequests();
  }

  // Update local bucket with Redis truth
  localBuckets.set(userId, {
    tokens: limit.max - result.current,
    lastRefill: Date.now(),
  });
});

// Dynamic configuration via Redis
const TIER_LIMITS_KEY = 'config:rate-limits';
let cachedLimits: Record = {};

// Refresh config every 30 seconds
setInterval(async () => {
  const config = await redis.hGetAll(TIER_LIMITS_KEY);
  if (Object.keys(config).length > 0) {
    cachedLimits = Object.fromEntries(
      Object.entries(config).map(([tier, json]) => [tier, JSON.parse(json)])
    );
  }
}, 30000);

// Admin endpoint to update limits without deploy
app.put('/admin/rate-limits/:tier', async (req) => {
  const { tier } = req.params;
  const { windowMs, max } = req.body;
  await redis.hSet(TIER_LIMITS_KEY, tier, JSON.stringify({ windowMs, max }));
  return { message: `Rate limit for ${tier} updated to ${max} per ${windowMs}ms` };
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: At 500K users, Redis rate limiting was consuming 40% of Redis CPU. The sorted set operations (ZADD, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE, ZCARD) for the sliding window were expensive, with each check requiring 3 Redis commands even when batched in a Lua script.

Investigation: Manoj profiled Redis operations. With 50K active users × 2 req/s = 100K Lua eval/s. Each Lua script performed 3 sorted set operations on keys with up to 60 members. Total: 300K sorted set operations per second. Redis's single-threaded architecture was the bottleneck.

Fix: (1) Added local token bucket as fast path — for users with >50% budget remaining, skip Redis entirely. This eliminated 80% of Redis checks. (2) For the remaining 20%, used fixed-window counter (simple INCR + EXPIRE) instead of sorted set for most endpoints. Only used sliding window for sensitive endpoints (login, payment). (3) Redis load dropped from 40% to 8%. (4) Added Redis Cluster with hash-based sharding of rate limit keys across 3 nodes for future growth.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Redis becomes bottleneck for rate checksLocal fast-path for users with plenty of budget; skip Redis for 80% of checks
Sorted set operations expensive at scaleUse fixed-window counter (INCR) for most endpoints; sorted set only for critical
Configuration changes require deploymentRedis-stored config with 30s polling; admin API for real-time updates
Local and Redis state divergeSync local bucket from Redis result; treat Redis as source of truth

Validation Strategy

  • Throughput test: 100K rate checks/second, verify Redis CPU < 20%
  • Fast path efficiency: measure Redis command rate, verify 80% reduction with local bucket
  • Config change test: update limit via admin API, verify effective within 30 seconds
  • Accuracy test: send exactly max+1 requests, verify max+1th is rejected (with and without fast path)

Definition

Background Workers performance bottlenecks in Node.js arise from: event loop contention between HTTP handlers and background tasks, memory pressure from queued jobs, database connection pool sharing between workers and API, and lack of job prioritization causing critical tasks to wait behind bulk operations.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj ran background workers for: (1) sending approval notification emails (critical, must send within 1 min), (2) generating monthly leave reports (heavy, takes 30 min), (3) syncing leave data to the external payroll system (medium, every 15 min), (4) cleaning up expired tokens (lightweight, daily). All four ran in the same Node.js process as the API, causing the monthly report generation to block HTTP responses by consuming 80% of the event loop.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import { Queue, Worker, QueueScheduler } from 'bullmq';
import { createClient } from 'redis';

const connection = { host: 'localhost', port: 6379 };

// Priority queues
const emailQueue = new Queue('email-notifications', { connection });
const syncQueue = new Queue('payroll-sync', { connection });
const reportQueue = new Queue('monthly-reports', { connection });

// Email worker — HIGH priority, fast, runs in API process
const emailWorker = new Worker('email-notifications', async (job) => {
  const { to, subject, template, data } = job.data;
  await sendEmail(to, subject, renderTemplate(template, data));
  console.log(`Email sent to ${to}: ${subject}`);
}, {
  connection,
  concurrency: 10,       // Process 10 emails in parallel
  limiter: {
    max: 50,             // Max 50 per minute (email provider limit)
    duration: 60000,
  },
});

// Report worker — LOW priority, CPU-heavy, SEPARATE process
// run-report-worker.ts (deployed as separate Render service)
const reportWorker = new Worker('monthly-reports', async (job) => {
  const { departmentId, month, year } = job.data;

  // Use streaming to avoid loading all data into memory
  const cursor = prisma.leave.findMany({
    where: { departmentId, startDate: { gte: new Date(year, month - 1, 1) } },
    cursor: undefined,
    take: 1000, // Process in chunks
  });

  let summary = { total: 0, approved: 0, rejected: 0, pending: 0 };
  // Process chunks...
  for await (const chunk of streamQuery(cursor)) {
    chunk.forEach(leave => {
      summary.total++;
      summary[leave.status.toLowerCase()]++;
    });

    // Report progress
    await job.updateProgress(Math.round((summary.total / job.data.expectedTotal) * 100));
  }

  // Save report
  await prisma.report.create({ data: { departmentId, month, year, summary } });
  return summary;
}, {
  connection,
  concurrency: 2,        // Only 2 reports at a time (heavy)
});

// Enqueue from API
app.post('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (req) => {
  const leave = await approveLeave(req.params.id, req.user.id);

  // Enqueue notification — critical priority
  await emailQueue.add('approval-notification', {
    to: leave.employee.email,
    subject: 'Leave Approved',
    template: 'leave-approved',
    data: { name: leave.employee.name, dates: `${leave.startDate} - ${leave.endDate}` },
  }, { priority: 1 }); // Highest priority

  return leave;
});

// Schedule recurring jobs
await reportQueue.add('monthly-report', { month: new Date().getMonth() + 1, year: new Date().getFullYear() }, {
  repeat: { pattern: '0 2 1 * *' }, // 2 AM on 1st of each month
  priority: 10, // Low priority
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: On the 1st of every month, the HR API became unresponsive for 30 minutes. Users couldn't submit or approve leaves during this window.

Investigation: Manoj correlated the outage with the monthly report generation cron job. The report worker ran in the same Node.js process as the API server. It loaded all leave records for the month into memory (500K records × 2KB = 1GB), performed aggregation, and wrote the report. During this time, the event loop was blocked, and GC pauses exceeded 2 seconds.

Fix: (1) Moved report worker to a separate Render service (own process, own memory). (2) Changed from loading all records to streaming with cursor-based pagination (1000 records at a time). (3) Added job progress tracking — admin dashboard shows "Report: 45% complete." (4) Set memory limit on report worker process (--max-old-space-size=512) to prevent OOM. Result: API unaffected during report generation.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Background workers block API event loopRun CPU-heavy workers in separate processes
Workers consume all DB connectionsSeparate connection pools for API and workers
Failed jobs lost foreverBullMQ retry with exponential backoff; dead-letter queue after 3 failures
No visibility into job progressBullMQ progress tracking; admin dashboard with job status

Validation Strategy

  • Isolation test: run report generation while benchmarking API — API p99 should stay < 100ms
  • Memory test: process 500K records, verify worker memory stays under 512MB
  • Retry test: simulate email send failure, verify 3 retries with exponential backoff
  • Priority test: enqueue 100 low-priority reports + 1 high-priority email, verify email processed first

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Background Workers examines job queue patterns (priority queues, delayed jobs, recurring schedules), worker isolation strategies, exactly-once processing, failure handling (retries, dead-letter queues, compensation), resource management (concurrency limits, rate limiting), and rollback plans for stuck or poison jobs.

Concrete Use-Case

In ContactsHub, Manoj used background workers for: (1) CSV import processing (500 contacts/batch, async), (2) Cloudinary image resizing (generate thumbnails after upload), (3) Playwright test runs (periodic E2E health checks), (4) Data export (generate CSV/PDF for download). Each job type has different requirements: CSV imports need exactly-once processing (no duplicate contacts), image resizing is idempotent (safe to retry), and exports are user-facing (need progress tracking).

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import { Queue, Worker, FlowProducer } from 'bullmq';

// CSV Import with exactly-once processing
const importWorker = new Worker('csv-import', async (job) => {
  const { rows, userId, importId } = job.data;
  let imported = 0;
  let skipped = 0;
  const errors = [];

  for (const [index, row] of rows.entries()) {
    // Idempotency: use email as dedup key
    const idempotencyKey = `import:${importId}:${row.email}`;
    const alreadyProcessed = await redis.sIsMember('processed-imports', idempotencyKey);
    if (alreadyProcessed) { skipped++; continue; }

    try {
      await db.collection('contacts').insertOne({
        ...row,
        userId,
        importId,
        createdAt: new Date(),
      });
      await redis.sAdd('processed-imports', idempotencyKey);
      imported++;
    } catch (err) {
      if (err.code === 11000) { // Duplicate key
        skipped++;
      } else {
        errors.push({ row: index + 1, error: err.message });
      }
    }

    // Report progress
    await job.updateProgress(Math.round(((index + 1) / rows.length) * 100));
  }

  // Notify user via WebSocket
  io.to(`user:${userId}`).emit('import-complete', {
    importId,
    imported,
    skipped,
    errors: errors.slice(0, 50),
  });

  return { imported, skipped, errors: errors.length };
}, {
  connection,
  concurrency: 3,
  stalledInterval: 30000, // Detect stalled jobs after 30s
});

// Image resize — idempotent, safe to retry
const imageWorker = new Worker('image-resize', async (job) => {
  const { contactId, imageUrl } = job.data;

  // Idempotent: re-generating thumbnails is safe
  const thumbnails = await Promise.all([
    cloudinary.upload(imageUrl, { width: 40, height: 40, crop: 'fill', format: 'webp' }),
    cloudinary.upload(imageUrl, { width: 200, height: 200, crop: 'fill', format: 'webp' }),
  ]);

  await db.collection('contacts').updateOne(
    { _id: contactId },
    { $set: { thumbnailUrl: thumbnails[0].url, mediumUrl: thumbnails[1].url } },
  );
}, {
  connection,
  concurrency: 5,
  attempts: 3,
  backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 2000 },
});

// Export with progress tracking via SSE
app.get('/api/exports/:id/progress', async (req, reply) => {
  const job = await exportQueue.getJob(req.params.id);
  if (!job) throw fastify.httpErrors.notFound();

  reply.raw.writeHead(200, {
    'Content-Type': 'text/event-stream',
    'Cache-Control': 'no-cache',
    'Connection': 'keep-alive',
  });

  const interval = setInterval(async () => {
    const state = await job.getState();
    const progress = job.progress;
    reply.raw.write(`data: ${JSON.stringify({ state, progress })}\n\n`);

    if (state === 'completed' || state === 'failed') {
      clearInterval(interval);
      reply.raw.end();
    }
  }, 1000);

  req.raw.on('close', () => clearInterval(interval));
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: ContactsHub's CSV import occasionally created duplicate contacts. A user imported 500 contacts, but 520 appeared in their list — 20 duplicates.

Investigation: Manoj traced the duplicates to the import worker crashing mid-batch (Cloudinary connection error during thumbnail generation). BullMQ retried the entire job from the beginning, re-importing the first 200 contacts that were already inserted before the crash.

Fix: (1) Added per-row idempotency using a Redis Set of processed import keys (importId:email). (2) Before inserting, check if the key exists in the processed set. (3) On successful insert, add to the processed set. (4) Separated thumbnail generation into a separate job (chained via BullMQ FlowProducer) — import completes first, then thumbnails are generated as follow-up jobs. Result: zero duplicates even on retry.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Job retry creates duplicatesPer-item idempotency key; check before processing
Stalled job blocks queueBullMQ stalledInterval (30s); auto-requeue after timeout
Poison job fails repeatedlyMax 3 retries → move to dead-letter queue → admin alert
No visibility into long-running jobsProgress tracking via job.updateProgress(); SSE or WebSocket for user-facing

Validation Strategy

  • Idempotency test: process same CSV twice, verify zero duplicates
  • Stalled test: kill worker mid-job, verify job auto-retries within 30s
  • Progress test: start 500-row import, verify WebSocket receives progress updates 0→100%
  • DLQ test: submit job that always fails, verify moves to dead-letter after 3 retries

Definition

Architecting File Streaming for 1M+ users means designing a system that handles concurrent file uploads and downloads without exhausting server memory, using Node.js streams to process data in chunks. Key concerns: backpressure handling, memory bounds for large files, concurrent stream limits, and efficient proxying to cloud storage.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled ContactsHub, users upload profile images (up to 10MB) and CSV files (up to 50MB for bulk imports). With 1M users, peak upload concurrency could reach 1000 simultaneous uploads. Manoj would stream files directly from the client to Cloudinary (images) or process CSVs through a streaming parser without loading the entire file into memory. Download: generate CSV exports of 50K contacts streaming from MongoDB cursor to HTTP response.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import { pipeline } from 'stream/promises';
import { Transform } from 'stream';
import csvParser from 'csv-parser';

// Image upload: stream directly to Cloudinary (no server buffering)
app.post('/api/contacts/:id/avatar', async (req, reply) => {
  const file = await req.file(); // Fastify multipart stream
  if (!file) throw fastify.httpErrors.badRequest('No file');

  // Validate before streaming
  if (!['image/jpeg', 'image/png', 'image/webp'].includes(file.mimetype)) {
    throw fastify.httpErrors.badRequest('Invalid image type');
  }

  // Stream directly to Cloudinary — never loads full file into memory
  const result = await new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const uploadStream = cloudinary.uploader.upload_stream(
      {
        folder: 'contacts',
        public_id: req.params.id,
        transformation: [{ width: 400, height: 400, crop: 'fill' }],
      },
      (error, result) => error ? reject(error) : resolve(result),
    );

    // pipeline() handles backpressure automatically
    pipeline(file.file, uploadStream).catch(reject);
  });

  await db.collection('contacts').updateOne(
    { _id: req.params.id },
    { $set: { avatarUrl: result.secure_url } },
  );

  return { avatarUrl: result.secure_url };
});

// CSV import: streaming parser with backpressure
app.post('/api/contacts/import', async (req, reply) => {
  const file = await req.file();
  if (!file) throw fastify.httpErrors.badRequest('No file');

  let processed = 0;
  let errors = 0;
  const batch = [];
  const BATCH_SIZE = 100;

  const insertBatch = async (rows) => {
    try {
      await db.collection('contacts').insertMany(rows, { ordered: false });
    } catch (err) {
      errors += err.writeErrors?.length || 1;
    }
  };

  // Transform stream: collect rows into batches, insert when full
  const batcher = new Transform({
    objectMode: true,
    async transform(row, encoding, callback) {
      batch.push({ ...row, userId: req.user.id, createdAt: new Date() });
      processed++;

      if (batch.length >= BATCH_SIZE) {
        await insertBatch(batch.splice(0, batch.length));
      }
      callback();
    },
    async flush(callback) {
      if (batch.length > 0) await insertBatch(batch);
      callback();
    },
  });

  await pipeline(file.file, csvParser(), batcher);

  return { processed, errors };
});

// Download: Stream MongoDB cursor to CSV response
app.get('/api/contacts/export', async (req, reply) => {
  const cursor = db.collection('contacts')
    .find({ userId: req.user.id })
    .project({ name: 1, email: 1, phone: 1, company: 1 });

  reply.header('Content-Type', 'text/csv');
  reply.header('Content-Disposition', 'attachment; filename="contacts.csv"');

  // Write CSV header
  reply.raw.write('Name,Email,Phone,Company\n');

  // Stream each document as a CSV row
  for await (const contact of cursor) {
    const row = `"${contact.name}","${contact.email}","${contact.phone || ''}","${contact.company || ''}"\n`;
    const canContinue = reply.raw.write(row);
    if (!canContinue) {
      // Backpressure: wait for drain before continuing
      await new Promise(resolve => reply.raw.once('drain', resolve));
    }
  }

  reply.raw.end();
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: ContactsHub crashed with OOM when 50 users simultaneously uploaded 10MB images. The server had 512MB memory but consumed 2.5GB during the upload spike.

Investigation: Manoj checked the upload handler — it used req.body which buffered the entire multipart form into memory. With 50 concurrent uploads × 50MB (file + base64 overhead) = 2.5GB. The file was then re-read from the buffer to upload to Cloudinary, doubling memory usage.

Fix: (1) Switched to @fastify/multipart with stream mode — each file is a readable stream, not buffered. (2) Used pipeline() to pipe the stream directly to Cloudinary's upload stream. (3) Memory per upload: 64KB (Node stream highWaterMark) instead of 10MB. (4) 50 concurrent uploads: 50 × 64KB = 3.2MB instead of 2.5GB. (5) Added a concurrency limiter: max 100 simultaneous uploads, queue the rest.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Buffering entire file into memoryUse stream mode for multipart uploads; pipe directly to destination
Backpressure ignored → memory growsUse pipeline() which handles backpressure automatically
Slow destination blocks fast uploadsConnection timeout per upload (60s); reject if destination is too slow
CSV export for large datasets is slowStream from DB cursor; handle drain events for backpressure

Validation Strategy

  • Memory test: upload 100 × 10MB files concurrently, verify process memory stays under 200MB
  • Backpressure test: throttle Cloudinary to 1MB/s, upload 10MB file — verify no memory spike
  • Export test: export 100K contacts as CSV, verify memory stays under 100MB (streaming)
  • Timeout test: simulate Cloudinary timeout, verify upload fails gracefully within 60s

Definition

File Streaming performance bottlenecks in Node.js arise from: buffer accumulation when backpressure is ignored, transform stream CPU overhead for parsing/encoding, connection pool exhaustion for concurrent uploads to external services, and GC pressure from allocating many small buffers.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj's monthly report export generated a 200MB CSV file from PostgreSQL containing all leave records across 500 departments. The bottleneck: Prisma loaded all records into memory (2GB with JavaScript object overhead), converted to CSV, then sent as a single response. With 5 concurrent report downloads, the server consumed 10GB and crashed.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Streaming CSV export with PostgreSQL cursor
import { Readable } from 'stream';
import { pipeline } from 'stream/promises';

// Create a readable stream from PostgreSQL cursor
function createPGCursorStream(prisma: PrismaClient, query: string, batchSize = 1000): Readable {
  let cursorName = `export_cursor_${Date.now()}`;
  let done = false;

  return new Readable({
    objectMode: true,
    async read() {
      if (done) { this.push(null); return; }

      try {
        if (!this._cursorDeclared) {
          await prisma.$executeRawUnsafe(`DECLARE ${cursorName} CURSOR FOR ${query}`);
          this._cursorDeclared = true;
        }

        const rows = await prisma.$queryRawUnsafe(
          `FETCH ${batchSize} FROM ${cursorName}`
        );

        if (rows.length === 0) {
          await prisma.$executeRawUnsafe(`CLOSE ${cursorName}`);
          done = true;
          this.push(null);
          return;
        }

        for (const row of rows) {
          this.push(row);
        }
      } catch (err) {
        this.destroy(err);
      }
    },
  });
}

// CSV transform stream
function createCSVTransform(headers: string[]) {
  let headerWritten = false;

  return new Transform({
    objectMode: true,
    transform(row, encoding, callback) {
      if (!headerWritten) {
        this.push(headers.join(',') + '\n');
        headerWritten = true;
      }
      const csvRow = headers.map(h => {
        const val = String(row[h] || '').replace(/"/g, '""');
        return `"${val}"`;
      }).join(',') + '\n';
      callback(null, csvRow);
    },
  });
}

// Export endpoint with streaming
app.get('/api/reports/leaves/export', async (req, reply) => {
  const { departmentId, month, year } = req.query;

  reply.header('Content-Type', 'text/csv');
  reply.header('Content-Disposition', `attachment; filename="leaves-${month}-${year}.csv"`);
  reply.header('Transfer-Encoding', 'chunked');

  const cursorStream = createPGCursorStream(
    prisma,
    `SELECT e.name, l.start_date, l.end_date, l.type, l.status, l.days
     FROM leave_request l
     JOIN employee e ON l.employee_id = e.id
     WHERE l.department_id = '${departmentId}'
     AND EXTRACT(MONTH FROM l.start_date) = ${month}
     AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM l.start_date) = ${year}
     ORDER BY l.start_date`,
    1000,
  );

  const csvTransform = createCSVTransform(
    ['name', 'start_date', 'end_date', 'type', 'status', 'days']
  );

  await pipeline(cursorStream, csvTransform, reply.raw);
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: The monthly leave report export took 5 minutes for a large department (10K records) and timed out for the full company (500K records). The Render service restarted due to OOM during company-wide exports.

Investigation: Manoj profiled the export: Prisma's findMany() loaded all 500K records into memory, created JavaScript objects for each (2KB per record = 1GB), then iterated to build a CSV string (another 500MB). Total: 1.5GB for a single export. The 512MB Render instance couldn't handle it.

Fix: (1) Replaced Prisma findMany with a PostgreSQL cursor that fetches 1000 rows at a time. (2) Created a Node.js Readable stream wrapping the cursor. (3) Piped through a CSV Transform stream that converts each row to a CSV line. (4) Piped directly to the HTTP response with chunked transfer encoding. Memory per export: cursor buffer (1000 × 2KB = 2MB) + stream buffers (64KB) = ~10MB. (5) Added a concurrency limit: max 3 concurrent exports. Result: company-wide export completes in 30 seconds using 10MB memory.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
ORM loads all results into memoryUse raw SQL cursor for large result sets; stream row by row
Transform stream creates GC pressureReuse buffers; avoid creating new strings for each row
Client disconnect doesn't stop server processingListen for close event on request; destroy cursor on disconnect
Concurrent exports exhaust DB connectionsConcurrency limit (semaphore); each cursor holds 1 connection

Validation Strategy

  • Memory test: export 500K rows, verify process memory stays under 50MB
  • Throughput test: 3 concurrent exports of 100K rows each, verify all complete within 60s
  • Disconnect test: start export, disconnect client mid-stream — verify cursor closed, connection returned
  • Backpressure test: slow client (100KB/s), verify server memory doesn't grow during 500K row export

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Security Headers examines HTTP security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy), their trade-offs (security vs. functionality), failure modes (CSP blocking legitimate scripts, HSTS preload lock-in), implementation strategies for SPAs, and rollback plans when security headers break production.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj configured security headers for the Angular SPA served by Fastify: strict CSP to prevent XSS, HSTS for HTTPS enforcement, X-Frame-Options DENY to prevent clickjacking, and a Permissions-Policy to disable unnecessary browser APIs (camera, geolocation, microphone). The challenge: the Angular app uses inline styles from Angular Material and loads Recharts from a CDN — both need CSP exceptions.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import helmet from '@fastify/helmet';

// Security headers configuration
app.register(helmet, {
  contentSecurityPolicy: {
    useDefaults: false,
    directives: {
      defaultSrc: ["'self'"],
      scriptSrc: ["'self'", 'cdn.recharts.com'],
      styleSrc: ["'self'", "'unsafe-inline'"], // Angular Material requires inline styles
      imgSrc: ["'self'", 'res.cloudinary.com', 'data:'],
      connectSrc: ["'self'", 'wss:', process.env.API_URL], // WebSocket + API
      fontSrc: ["'self'", 'fonts.googleapis.com', 'fonts.gstatic.com'],
      frameSrc: ["'none'"],
      objectSrc: ["'none'"],
      baseUri: ["'self'"],
      formAction: ["'self'"],
      reportUri: '/api/csp-violations',
    },
  },
  hsts: {
    maxAge: 31536000,      // 1 year
    includeSubDomains: true,
    preload: false,         // Don't preload until confirmed stable
  },
  frameguard: { action: 'deny' },
  noSniff: true,
  referrerPolicy: { policy: 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin' },
  permissionsPolicy: {
    features: {
      camera: ["'none'"],
      microphone: ["'none'"],
      geolocation: ["'none'"],
      payment: ["'none'"],
    },
  },
});

// CSP violation reporting endpoint
app.post('/api/csp-violations', async (req) => {
  const violation = req.body['csp-report'] || req.body;
  console.warn('CSP Violation:', {
    blockedUri: violation['blocked-uri'],
    violatedDirective: violation['violated-directive'],
    documentUri: violation['document-uri'],
    sourceFile: violation['source-file'],
    lineNumber: violation['line-number'],
  });

  // Track metrics
  cspViolationCounter.increment({
    directive: violation['violated-directive'],
    blockedUri: violation['blocked-uri'],
  });

  return { received: true };
});

// Report-only mode for testing new CSP rules
app.addHook('onSend', (req, reply, payload, done) => {
  if (process.env.CSP_MODE === 'report-only') {
    // Move CSP to report-only header
    const csp = reply.getHeader('content-security-policy');
    if (csp) {
      reply.removeHeader('content-security-policy');
      reply.header('content-security-policy-report-only', csp);
    }
  }
  done();
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After deploying strict CSP headers, the HR dashboard's Recharts graphs stopped rendering. Users saw blank chart areas. No errors appeared in the Fastify server logs.

Investigation: Manoj checked the browser console — CSP violations: "Refused to execute inline script because it violates the Content-Security-Policy directive: script-src 'self' cdn.recharts.com". Recharts used inline <script> tags for initialization. The CSP allowed the CDN domain but not inline scripts.

Fix: (1) Added a nonce-based CSP for scripts: generate a unique nonce per request, inject into the CSP header and the Recharts script tag. (2) For the immediate fix, deployed in report-only mode (no blocking) while testing the nonce approach. (3) Set up CSP violation monitoring — violations sent to /api/csp-violations and tracked as metrics. (4) After 7 days of zero legitimate violations in report-only mode, switched to enforcement mode. (5) Added a feature flag: CSP_MODE=report-only to instantly switch back if issues arise.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
CSP blocks legitimate scripts/stylesDeploy in report-only mode first; monitor violations for 7 days
HSTS preload is irreversible for monthsStart without preload; only add after confirming HTTPS is permanent
Inline styles required by CSS-in-JS frameworksUse nonce-based CSP or hash-based CSP for specific inline styles
CSP violations flood monitoringRate limit violation reports; aggregate by directive and blocked URI

Validation Strategy

  • Report-only test: deploy CSP in report-only, verify zero legitimate violations for 7 days
  • Enforcement test: switch to enforcement, verify all app features still work
  • XSS test: attempt to inject <script>alert(1)</script> in user input — verify blocked by CSP
  • Clickjacking test: embed app in iframe — verify X-Frame-Options DENY blocks it

Definition

Architecting Security Headers for 1M+ users requires a header strategy that works across multiple deployment targets (CDN, origin servers, service workers), handles dynamic content (user-generated content, third-party embeds), supports CSP nonce generation at scale without performance overhead, and provides monitoring for security header effectiveness.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled CRM Sales Dashboard serving 1M users through a CDN, Manoj configured security headers at two layers: (1) CDN edge (Cloudflare) for static headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Permissions-Policy), (2) Origin server (Express) for dynamic headers (CSP with per-request nonce, CORS with origin validation). The challenge: the CDN caches responses, but CSP nonces must be unique per request — requiring CDN cache bypass for HTML pages while caching static assets.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Two-layer security headers
// Layer 1: Static headers (set at CDN or reverse proxy)
const STATIC_HEADERS = {
  'Strict-Transport-Security': 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains',
  'X-Frame-Options': 'DENY',
  'X-Content-Type-Options': 'nosniff',
  'Referrer-Policy': 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin',
  'Permissions-Policy': 'camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=()',
};

// Layer 2: Dynamic CSP with nonce (per HTML request)
app.addHook('onRequest', (req, reply, done) => {
  // Generate nonce for this request
  req.cspNonce = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString('base64');
  done();
});

app.addHook('onSend', (req, reply, payload, done) => {
  // Add static headers to all responses
  Object.entries(STATIC_HEADERS).forEach(([key, value]) => {
    reply.header(key, value);
  });

  // Add dynamic CSP only to HTML responses
  const contentType = reply.getHeader('content-type') || '';
  if (contentType.includes('text/html')) {
    const nonce = req.cspNonce;
    reply.header('Content-Security-Policy', [
      `default-src 'self'`,
      `script-src 'self' 'nonce-${nonce}'`,
      `style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' fonts.googleapis.com`,
      `img-src 'self' res.cloudinary.com data:`,
      `connect-src 'self' wss: ${process.env.API_URL}`,
      `font-src 'self' fonts.gstatic.com`,
      `frame-src 'none'`,
      `object-src 'none'`,
      `base-uri 'self'`,
      `form-action 'self'`,
      `report-uri /api/csp-violations`,
    ].join('; '));

    // Inject nonce into HTML template
    if (typeof payload === 'string' && payload.includes('')) {
      payload = payload.replace(
        '',
        ``
      );
    }
  }

  done(null, payload);
});

// CORS with validated origin list
const ALLOWED_ORIGINS = new Set([
  'https://crm.company.com',
  'https://admin.company.com',
  ...(process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development' ? ['http://localhost:4200'] : []),
]);

app.register(require('@fastify/cors'), {
  origin: (origin, callback) => {
    if (!origin || ALLOWED_ORIGINS.has(origin)) {
      callback(null, true);
    } else {
      callback(new Error('CORS rejected'), false);
    }
  },
  credentials: true,
  methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'PATCH', 'DELETE'],
  allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization', 'X-Correlation-ID'],
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After enabling CSP with nonces, the CDN started returning cached HTML pages with stale nonces. Each user received the same nonce from the CDN cache, and their browser's inline scripts failed CSP checks because the nonce didn't match the server-generated one.

Investigation: Manoj found that the CDN was caching HTML responses (Cache-Control: public, max-age=300). The CSP nonce in the cached response was from the first request. All subsequent users got the same nonce, but their browser expected a fresh one for each page load.

Fix: (1) Set Cache-Control: no-store for HTML pages (only cache static assets). (2) Added Vary: Cookie header so the CDN doesn't serve the same HTML to different authenticated users. (3) Static assets (JS, CSS, images) continue to be cached with Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable. (4) Added a CDN rule: if Content-Type: text/html, always pass to origin (no caching).

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
CDN caches HTML with stale CSP nonceNo-cache for HTML; only cache static assets with immutable
CORS wildcard allows any originExplicit origin allow-list; validate against Set for O(1) lookup
Security headers break third-party integrationsTest in report-only mode; whitelist specific domains in CSP
Nonce generation adds per-request overheadcrypto.randomBytes(16) is 0.01ms — negligible at any scale

Validation Strategy

  • Cache test: request HTML twice, verify different CSP nonce each time (no CDN caching)
  • CSP enforcement: inject XSS payload, verify browser blocks it (check violation report)
  • CORS test: request from unauthorized origin, verify rejected
  • Header audit: run Mozilla Observatory scan, target A+ grade
